meta.title: Perforin and Fas in murine gammaherpesvirus-specific CD8+ T cell control and morbidity

abstractHtml: The immune system uses both virus-specific T cells and B cells to control the acute and latent phases of respiratory infection with the murine gammaherpesvirus 68 (γHV-68). We sought to further define the important effector mechanisms for CD8<sup>+</sup> T cells. First, depletion of the CD4<sup>+</sup> T cells resulted in a failure of most animals to drive the virus into latency, although lytic virus in the lung was reduced by approximately 1000-fold from its peak. Second, the absence of either perforin or Fas alone had no impact on the ability to reduce titres of lytic virus in the lung. Further neutralization of IFN-γ in CD4-depleted P<sup>+/+</sup>, P<sup>-/-</sup> or Fas<sup>-/-</sup> mice had no effect. To define the requirements for Fas or perforin more clearly, two sets of chimeric mice were constructed differing in perforin expression by the T cells, and Fas on infected epithelial cells or lymphocytes. Animals with P<sup>-/-</sup> T cells and a Fas<sup>-/-</sup> lung failed to limit the shedding of infectious virus, regardless of whether CD4 T cells were present. In addition, we noted that having P<sup>-/-</sup> T cells in irradiated Fas<sup>+/+</sup> hosts caused a lethal disease that was not apparent in the non-chimeric (unirradiated) P<sup>-/-</sup> (Fas<sup>+/+</sup>) mice. In another set of chimeric mice, P<sup>-/-</sup> T cells were able to limit persistent infection of B cells that expressed Fas, but not B cells that were Fas-deficient. These studies demonstrate that some degree of cytotoxicity via either perforin or Fas is essential for CD8<sup>+</sup> T cells to control this DNA virus.
bodyHtml:
The antiviral effector mechanisms utilized by virus-specific CD8+ T cells may vary depending on the type of pathogen, the organ that is infected and the route of inoculation (Tay & Welsh, 1997 ). Evidence from various experimental systems indicates that virus-specific CD8 T cells can limit virus replication by secreting IFN-γ, lysing the infected cells or some combination of the two (Cavanaugh et al., 1998 ; Doherty et al., 1997 ; Fleck et al., 1998 ; Guidotti et al., 1994 ; Johnson et al., 1999 ; Kagi et al., 1994 , 1995a , b, 1996 ; Mullbacher et al., 1999a ). Lysis of the infected cells, in turn, can be accomplished through the targeted secretion of perforin and granzyme, or by engagement of the Fas receptor on the target cells by FasL on the CD8+ CTL (Kagi et al., 1995b ; Rosen et al., 2000 ; Topham et al., 1997 ; White et al., 1999 ). Perforin-deficient (P-/-) mice can effectively control a number of different virus infections (Franco et al., 1997 ; Kagi et al., 1995b ), while in other infections perforin is essential (Johnson et al., 1999 ; Mullbacher et al., 1999a ; Ramshaw et al., 1997 ; Rossi et al., 1998 ). The mode of action of these P-/- CD8+ T cells is obviously of considerable interest (Lowin et al., 1994 b; Weck et al., 1999 ). Analysis of a transgenic mouse model of human hepatitis B infection has shown that IFN-γ production by the virus-specific CD8 T cells is enormously important (Cavanaugh et al., 1998 ; Guidotti et al., 1994 ). However, this situation seems to be a peculiar feature of the infectious process in the liver, reflecting the sensitivity of the hepatocytes to IFN-γ (Guidotti et al., 1999 ; Schultz & Chisari, 1999 ), and illustrating the organ-dependence of antiviral mechanisms (Tay & Welsh, 1997 ). The P-/- mice also deal effectively with acute influenza virus infection of the lung, even when the CD4+ T cell subset is depleted (Doherty et al., 1997 ; Topham et al., 1997 ). Experiments with radiation bone marrow chimeras demonstrated that the control of influenza in the lung by the P-/- CD8+ T cells was dependent on the expression of Fas on the host respiratory mucosa (Topham et al., 1997 ). This is supported by the observation that influenza virus up-regulates Fas expression by cultured lung epithelial cells (Takizawa et al., 1995 ), while another negative-strand RNA virus (respiratory syncytial virus) was shown to up-regulate Fas on respiratory epithelial cells in vivo (ODonnell et al., 1999 ).

What then are the relative roles of perforin and Fas in the CD8+ T cell-mediated control of respiratory infection with a large DNA virus? Intranasal challenge with murine gammaherpesvirus 68 (γHV-68) (Cardin et al., 1996 ; Nash & Sunil-Chandra, 1994 ; Virgin & Speck, 1999 ) is characterized by a lytic phase in epithelial cells (particularly of the respiratory tract) and a persistent, latent infection of B lymphocytes and macrophages (Cardin et al., 1996 ; Sunil-Chandra et al., 1992 ; Virgin & Speck, 1999 ; Weck et al., 1999 ). Depletion experiments in B cell-deficient µMT and B6 mice have demonstrated that the infection can be controlled by either CD4+ or CD8+ T cells, but not when T cells are absent (Christensen et al., 1999 ; Stevenson et al., 1999c ). Control of the infection in P-/- mice was shown to differ little from P+/+ mice, leading to the conclusion that the perforin/granzyme pathway is not required to deal with this virus (Usherwood et al., 1997 ). However, these experiments did not consider the role of the CD4+ T cells. More recent experiments showed that CD4+ T cells are important antiviral effector cells for this infection, and operate through IFN-γ (Christensen et al., 1999 ). Whether CD8+ T cells can operate via a similar mechanism, or are dependent on another cytokine or cytotoxic process, is not known. The current analysis focuses on the control of lytic γHV-68 infection in the lung by CD8+ T cells operating in the absence of the CD4+ subset. The contribution of IFN-γ was investigated in B6 (P+/+), P-/- and Fas-/- congenic mice, while chimeric animals with P+/+ or P-/- T cells and Fas+/+ or Fas-/- infected target cells were used to dissect the requirements for perforin- and Fas-mediated cytotoxicity.

Animals.
C57BL6/J (B6), congenic C57BL6/Thy1.1/IgHa and Fas-/- B6.MRLlpr/lpr (lpr) mice were purchased from Jackson Laboratories (Bar Harbour, ME, USA). Breeding pairs of the perforin-deficient (P-/-) strain of mice back-crossed to the B6 background were a kind gift of Hans Hengartner (Zürich, Switzerland) and bred at St Jude Childrens Research Hospital (Memphis, TN, USA). All animals were maintained at St Jude Childrens Research Hospital under specific pathogen-free conditions until infection with γHV-68.

Virus, infection and sampling.
γHV-68 (clone G2.4), obtained from A. A. Nash (Edinburgh, UK), was grown in owl monkey kidney cells [OMK; CRL 1566 (ATCC)] (Cardin et al., 1996 ). For infection, animals were anaesthetized with Avertin (2,2,2-tribromoethanol) prior to intranasal (i.n.) inoculation with 104 p.f.u. γHV-68 in 30 µl PBS. After infection, at the times indicated, the animals were given a lethal dose of Avertin and bled from the axilla. The inflammatory cells from the lung were obtained by broncheoalveolar lavage (BAL) (Allan et al., 1990 ), and the draining mediastinal lymph node (MLN) and the spleen were made into a single cell suspensions for analysis. Lungs were removed and frozen at -70 °C for later titration of infectious virus.

Titration of infectious and latent virus in tissue samples.
The presence of infectious virus, or cells releasing infectious virus, was determined by plaque assay on NIH 3T3 cells (ATCC CRL 1658) (Cardin et al., 1996 ). Briefly, lung tissue was first disrupted in a Dounce homogenizer, and then frozen and thawed three time to lyse the cells and release virus. Dilutions of the homogenate were then adsorbed onto 3T3 monolayers for 1 h at 37 °C, overlaid with carboxymethylcellulose (CMC), and incubated at 37 °C for 5 days, At the end of the incubation, the overlay was removed, and the cells were fixed with methanol and stained with Giemsa prior to enumeration of the plaques. To determine the number of latently infected cells in the spleen, an infectious centre (i.c.) assay was performed by co-culturing various numbers of the spleen cells with 5x105 NIH 3T3 cells at 37 °C under a CMC overlay for 6 days, followed by removal of the overlay, and then fixation and staining with methanol and Giemsa. Aliquots of spleen cells were also sonicated to disrupt the cells and assayed for infectious virus.

Monoclonal antibody treatment and flow cytometry.
CD4 T cells were depleted by treatment of the animals with the GK1.5 MAb to CD4, and IFN-γ was neutralized by treatment with the XMG1.2 MAb (Christensen et al., 1999 ; Stevenson et al., 1998 ). Treatment was begun 5 days prior to infection and continued on alternating days throughout the course of the experiment. The prevalence of various lymphocyte subsets and the efficacy of the depletion regimen were examined by flow cytometry. Briefly, single cell suspensions were stained with phycoerythrin (PE)-conjugated 53-6.72 MAb to CD8 and FITC-conjugated RM-4-4 MAb to CD4, or PE-conjugated 30-H12 MAb to Thy1.2 and FITC-conjugated RA3-6B2 MAb to B220 prior to analysis on a FACScan (Becton Dickinson) using CellQuest software. In vitro T cell depletions and B cell depletions were similarly analysed by staining the cells with B220FITC and PE-conjugated 2C11 to CD3 to confirm removal of the appropriate subset. For identification of Ig heavy chain allotype expression, the cells were stained with FITC or PE conjugated DS-1 MAb to mouse IgMa and AF6-78 MAb to mouse IgMb. All conjugated antibodies were obtained from Pharmingen.

Radiation chimeras.
To study control of infection in the lung, Fas+/+ B6 mice and Fas-/- lpr mice were lethally irradiated with 950 rad and reconstituted 24 h later with 2x107 BM cells from either P+/+ or P-/- naïve mice. The animals were rested for 12 weeks to allow reconstitution of the immune cells before infection with γ-HV68. As above, beginning 1 day prior to infection and continuing every 2 to 3 days, some of the animals were treated with GK1.5 to remove the CD4 T cells, and XMG1.2 MAb to neutralize IFN-γ (Christensen et al., 1999 ; Topham et al., 1997 ). To study the control of persistently infected B cells, two additional sets of chimeric animals were constructed by reconstituting irradiated (950 rad) B6 mice with 1x107 T cell-depleted Fas+/+ B6 BM or Fas-/- lpr BM. Two weeks later, the animals received 5x107 B cell-depleted spleen cells from perforin intact B6 or P-/- naïve mice. The T-depleted bone marrow was prepared by staining the BM with the anti-Thy1.2 IgM MAb AT83 prior to lysis with a 1:5 mixture of rabbit and guinea pig complement (Cedar Lane Laboratories, Ontario, Canada) (Topham & Doherty, 1998 ). The efficacy of the T cell depletion was assessed by FACS subsequent to staining with MAb to CD3ε (2C11) and was >99%. The depletion of the B cells from the donor spleens was accomplished by staining the cells with anti-class II MHC MAb to IAb (ATCC TIB120) followed by incubation of the cells with a mixture of anti-rat Ig and anti-mouse Ig magnetic beads (Dynal), and later harvesting the non-adherent cells after placing against a magnet. The remaining cells were <1% B220+ at the end of the protocol. In a second experiment, the same techniques of enrichment were employed except that the recipients were thymectomized, congenic C57BL6/Thy1.1/IgHa mice which differ in their Ig heavy chain allotype expression (IgHa vs IgHb).

Intracellular IFN-γ staining.
The non-adherent BAL cell population was cultured for 6 h in 96-well round-bottom plates at 58x105 cells per well in complete medium containing 10 µg/ml Brefeldin A (Epicenter Technologies, Madison, WI, USA) with or without 110 µM γHV-68-specific peptides, p56 or p79 (Stevenson et al., 1998 , 1999a ). After culture, the cells were placed on ice, washed in PBSBrefeldin A (10 µg/ml), stained with anti-CD8FITC as above, washed again, and fixed with 1% formaldehyde. The cells were permeabilized in 0·5% saponin (Sigma) before staining with anti-IFN-γPE (Pharmingen) for 30 min on ice. The cells were then washed and analysed on the FACScan as above.

CD8+ T cell-mediated control in mice lacking Fas or perforin
The acute lytic phase of γHV-68 infection in the respiratory epithelium was controlled with equal efficacy in P+/+Fas+/+ (B6), P-/-Fas+/+ (perforin-deficient B6) and P+/+Fas-/- (lpr) mice (Fig. 1ac). To observe the action of the CD8+ T cells in the absence of CD4+ T cells, animals were depleted of CD4+ T cells (Allan et al., 1990 ; Topham et al., 1997 ) by MAb treatment (Fig. 1df, Table 1). As shown previously in B6 mice (Christensen et al., 1999 ), the absence of the CD4+ subset delayed virus clearance in every case, with evidence of productive infection detectable in some mice until day 40, the latest time-point sampled (Fig. 1df). Even so, it was clear that the effector CD8+ T cells functioning in the absence of the CD4+ subset were able to control the lytic infection in B6, P-/- and lpr (Fas-/-) mice, reducing titres by about 1000-fold and maintaining that level for at least 3 weeks (Fig. 1df). As with other herpesviruses, it is clear that CD4+ T cells contribute significantly to the control of lytic γ-HV68 infection (Christensen et al., 1999 ; Christensen & Doherty, 1999 ; Stevenson et al., 1998 , 1999c ). Further compromising the CD8+ T cell response in CD4-depleted mice by the simultaneous neutralization of IFN-γ (Christensen et al., 1999 ) did not have a significant effect, though it appeared that fewer mice in each group reduced titres to undetectable levels (Fig. 1gi).


Table 1), while the XMG1.2 protocol has been shown previously to neutralize IFN-γ in the respiratory tract (Christensen et al., 1999 ).


Table 1. Consequences of depleting CD4+ T cells and IFN-γ from γHV-68-infected B6, P-/- and lpr mice


The control of persistent γHV-68 infection in the spleen was also analysed (Cardin et al., 1996 ; Nash & Sunil-Chandra, 1994 ; Virgin & Speck, 1999 ). Two weeks after virus challenge, when the number of latently infected cells reaches a peak (Cardin et al., 1996 ), unmanipulated B6, P-/- and lpr mice limited the extent of latent infection in the spleen to an equivalent degree (Table 1). However, the capacity of the CD8+ T cells to deal with this aspect of the infection was significantly reduced (P<0·001) in the lpr mice depleted of the CD4+ T cell subset and, in contrast to the lung, was further compromised by in vivo neutralization of IFN-γ (Table 1). There was some suggestion that the CD4- IFN-γ- P-/- mice had a higher number of infected cells (Table 1), but the difference from the CD4- P-/- group was not statistically significant in this experiment (Table 1) or in repeat experiments (data not shown). Taken together with the findings for the lpr mice, the results suggest that Fas is important for the CD8+ T cell-mediated control of persistently infected cells, while IFN-γ may enhance (at least) perforin-mediated effector functions.

Uncontrolled infection in the absence of perforin and Fas
What remains unclear from the analysis of P-/- and lpr mice are the individual contributions of Fas and perforin. IFN-γ does not appear to be an essential mediator of the antiviral activity of the cells remaining after CD4 T cell depletion, though there was some effect on latency in the lpr mice (Table 1, Fig. 1). The observation that γHV-68 is controlled in the lung regardless of whether the animals were missing either Fas or perforin alone suggested that keeping at least one of these modes of cytotoxic action intact is sufficient. Alternatively, there could be another mechanism of antiviral control (cytotoxic or otherwise) that had not yet been identified in these experiments. If there is a further means of control, this should emerge in a situation where both perforin and Fas are absent. We thus made radiation bone marrow chimeras using P-/- BM to provide the CD8+ CTL, and lpr mice as recipients or B cell donors to limit Fas expression either in the lung or B cells respectively. With such mice, the Fas pathway is retained among the lymphocytes to regulate T cell homeostasis.

To examine the issue of controlling lytic virus in the lung, lethally irradiated (950 rad) Fas+/+ (B6) or Fas-/- (lpr) recipients were reconstituted with P+/+ or P-/- bone marrow. The chimeras were held for 12 weeks to allow for T cell maturation, and then treated with anti-CD4 MAb (to deplete the CD4+ T cells) or a control rat Ig. At 10 and 15 days after infection (1314 weeks after reconstitution), the recipient animals had similar splenic cellularity and proportions of CD8+ T cells (Table 2). Analysis of the inflammatory exudate by BAL revealed a strong inflammatory cellular infiltrate that consisted of primarily CD8+ T cells (Table 2). Assaying the BAL cells for reactivity to the two most prominent class I MHC restricted epitopes of γHV-68 (p56 and p79) showed clearly that virus-specific CD8+ T cells were indeed present and responsive in all groups of the chimeric mice (Fig. 2).


Table 2. Reconstitution and control of latent infection in lung chimeras deficient in perforin and Fas



(48K):

 		Fig. 2. Functional virus-specific CD8+ T cells can be recovered by broncheoalveolar lavage of chimeric mice. The inflammatory cells infiltrating the lungs of CD4-depleted chimeras were sampled 15 days after i.n. inoculation with γHV-68 to assay for antigen-specific cells. Non-adherent (to plastic) BAL cells were stimulated in vitro with either the p56 or p79 γHV-68 derived peptides and analysed by flow cytometry for IFN-γ production using a well-established protocol (Stevenson et al., 1999a ) described in Methods. The percentage of IFN-γ-positive, CD8+ cells is indicated by the number in the upper right quadrant of each plot.

Elimination of infectious γHV-68 virus from the lungs of control P+/+→Fas+/+ (B6→B6) and P+/+→Fas-/- (B6→lpr) chimeras was similar (Fig. 3) to that observed in non-chimeric B6 and lpr mice (Fig. 1). As with the non-chimeric mice, elimination of the CD4+ T cells in these groups diminished virus control. It is notable that the group containing P-/- T cells and a Fas-/- lung (P-/-→lpr) (Fig. 3d) failed to limit lytic virus titres regardless of whether the CD4+ subset was present. Secondly, in the absence of the CD4+ T cells, the only group to reduce lytic virus titres below detection was the P+/+→Fas+/+ group (B6→B6) with 3/4 animals negative for virus at day 21 (Fig. 3a). The other groups of chimeric animals lacking either P+/+ T cells or a Fas+/+ lung were compromised. Lung virus titres at day 21 in CD4-depleted, Fas-/- mice with P+/+ T cells (B6→lpr) were no different than those measured at 15 days after infection (Fig. 3b). The poor control of virus in these chimeras was a phenomenon not evident in their non-chimeric counterparts (lpr) and may have been due to the reduced cellularity of the chimeras versus the unirradiated mice (Table 1). We were not able to assay the lungs of P-/-→B6 chimeric mice beyond day 18 because these animals succumbed (Fig. 3c). A separate experiment to assess the difference in mortality rates highlighted the increased susceptibility of the P-/-→B6 group (Fig. 4). The results suggests that the Fas-mediated elimination of infected cells is more promiscuous than perforin-mediated cytotoxicity, and that the lungs of irradiated mice may have enhanced sensitivity to this cytotoxic mode. In any case, it is clear that having both Fas and perforin cytotoxic mechanisms (Fig. 3a) is better than either one alone (Fig. 3b, c, and that the absence of both greatly compromises the control of the lytic infection (Fig. 3d), regardless of the contribution of the CD4+ subset. The failure to limit virus replication in the P-/-→lpr chimeras also rules out a major role for other antiviral cytotoxic mechanisms or cytokines that permit CD8+ T cells to control the lytic infection whether or not CD4 help is available.



(24K):

 		Fig. 3. Expression of either perforin or Fas is required to limit γHV-68 replication in the lung. Chimeric mice were made by transferring 2x107 P+/+ (a, b) or P-/- (c, d) BM cells into irradiated (950 rad) Fas+/+ B6 (a, c) or Fas-/- lpr (b, d) mice. The mice were held for 12 weeks to allow the maturation of T cell responsiveness, and then infected i.n. with 104 p.f.u. γHV-68 before the lungs were removed at intervals for titration. Filled symbols indicate animals that were depleted of CD4 T cells by treatment with GK1.5 as described in the legend to Fig. 1. Open symbols indicate results from control (not CD4-depleted) mice and are comparable to those obtained in early experiments (data not shown) with short-term chimeras reconstituted with 5x107 P+/+ or P-/- T cells and 107 P-/- BM cells, and infected within 96 h of cell transfer.


(21K):

 		Fig. 4. Increased morbidity in Fas+/+ chimeric mice reconstituted with P-/- T cells. A separate set of CD4-depleted chimeras, constructed as indicated in the legend to Fig. 3, were followed for 25 days after infection for signs of illness. Each group contained 11 to 15 animals.

Control of the persistent latent infection of B cells by perforin and Fas
The analysis with CD4-depleted lpr and P-/- mice indicated that cytotoxic effectors possibly function to limit γHV-68 persistence in lymphocytes via the recognition of Fas (Table 1). Other studies with CD8-depleted mice showed the latently infected B cells are controlled by CD4+ T cells via an IFN-γ-dependent mechanism (Christensen et al., 1999 ). Whether this effect was directly mediated by CD4+ T cells and the antiviral effects of IFN-γ, or indirectly through the recruitment of other effectors with cytotoxic function (NK cells) has yet to be determined. To examine the roles of Fas and perforin mediated cytotoxicity in this aspect of the infection, we constructed another set of chimeric mice using a similar strategy to that employed above, except in this situation the expression of Fas was varied within the B cell compartment rather than the lung. We used irradiated B6 (Fas+/+) recipients reconstituted with T-depleted Fas+/+ or Fas-/- BM for 2 weeks, and then given B cell depleted spleen cells from P+/+ or P-/- mice as a source of effector T cells (Table 3). In experiment 2 (Table 3), the recipient mice were also thymectomized prior to irradiation to preclude the de novo development of P+/+ effector T cells from the transferred BM. The latter groups also differed in the Ig allotype expression between the host environment and the donor BM so that the source of the latently infected B cells could be traced by flow cytometry. The first experiment (Table 3, expt 1) quite clearly showed that mice could control persistently infected lymphocytes in the absence of either Fas or perforin, but not when both were missing, or when no T cells had been transferred. The animals that received no T cells also failed to control the infection in the lung (data not shown). Similar results were obtained in the athymic recipients, where only the double deficient chimeras failed to control the latently infected cells (Table 3, expt 2). That the majority of the infected B cells were of donor origin, and not a residual population of radiation-resistant host cells, was confirmed by flow cytometric sorting for the donor allotype (IgHb) followed by assay for latent virus (Table 3, expt 2, results in brackets). The data show that, in the absence of the CD4+ subset, latently infected B cells are controlled by cytotoxic CD8+ T cells via perforin or Fas. There was no evidence for a CD8+ T cell-mediated non-cytolytic mechanism or a non-T cell antiviral population.


Table 3. Latently infected B cells are controlled by both Fas- and perforin-mediated mechanisms

The control of infection with this large, complex DNA virus clearly draws on a range of host response elements. How the various immune components work together is as yet far from understood, though some functions can now be assigned to individual lymphocyte subsets. The present experiments indicate that, even in the absence of CD4+ T cells, CD8+ CTL activity mediated via either the perforin or Fas pathways can cause substantial diminution of lytic virus titres in the lung, and a reduction in the number of latently infected lymphocytes. However, it is also clear from these experiments and others (Christensen et al., 1999 ) that a set of CD4+ effectors is operating in the intact mice. In the absence of B cells, these effectors have been shown to act directly via IFN-γ production (Christensen et al., 1999 ), but the experiments here also raise the possibility that they may, in addition, utilize Fas- or perforin-mediated cytotoxicity (Esser et al., 1997 ; Smyth et al., 1996 ; Vergelli et al., 1997 ; Williams & Engelhard, 1996 ). The potential involvement of cytotoxic effector mechanisms in CD4+ T cell-mediated control has not been pursued further in this analysis. The best way to do this may be to use cloned CD4+ and CD8+ T cells with defined cytokine and lytic potentials. Other experiments indicate that CD4+ T cell help for virus-specific antibody production by B cells is playing a major role in limiting reactivation of γHV-68 from latency (Stevenson et al., 1999c ; Stewart et al., 1998 ).

Another interpretation might consider whether CD4+ and CD8+ T cells act indirectly through the recruitment of natural killer (NK) cells. Although the potential of NK cells was not directly explored here, experiments done with doubly (CD4 and CD8) T-depleted mice (data not shown), or irradiated mice which received no T cells (Table 3), gave no evidence that NK cells are a major player in controlling γHV-68 respiratory infection. If NK cells do have a role, they are more likely to be important early (57 days) in the infection as they are for other viruses (Hussell & Openshaw, 1998 ; Sarawar et al., 1993 ; Tay et al., 1999 ), rather than the day 1040 time-points examined here. In fact, at 10 days after infection, the proportion of CD8+ T cells in the BAL of the chimeric animals was 8495%, leaving little room for NK cells. Even so, NK effector cells have been shown to use the perforin or, in tumour models, Fas-mediated cytotoxicity (Kodama et al., 1999 ; Rosen et al., 2000 ; Tay & Welsh, 1997 ; van den Broek et al., 1995 , 1996 ). Consequently, the NK effectors would also be compromised in the experimental models described here. Cytotoxicity alone, whether achieved through Fas, perforin or both has a limited capacity to eliminate the cells producing infectious virus from the lung (Doherty et al., 1997 ; Stevenson et al., 1999b ). This may be relevant to the situation where an individual continues to shed infectious virus while maintaining a degree of control that obscures disease. When both Fas and perforin are compromised as they are in the P-/-→Fas-/- chimeras, control of replicating virus is ineffectual, even in the presence of CD4+ T cells and Ig-secreting B cells. This suggests that some contact-dependent cytotoxic effector activity is always necessary, and that soluble cytotoxic mediators like TNF-α at best play an ancillary role for the control of this infection in the lung (Fleck et al., 1998 ; Liu et al., 1999 ). It still seems reasonable to assume that control of this virus (and certainly other viruses) by CD8+ T cells is enhanced by IFN-γ, in spite of the observation that it is not essential.

The high incidence of lethal disease seen consistently in P-/-→Fas+/+ chimeric mice raises questions about the specificity of Fas-directed CTL activity. We did not observe this consequence in unirradiated P-/- mice, which theoretically have similar cytotoxic machinery. That this lethal effect is an immunopathogenic consequence related to the expression of Fas is suggested by the fact that animals with the same P-/- cells in a Fas-/- host show no evidence of disease (Fig. 4). This is in spite of the fact that these mice do not control virus replication (Fig. 3). Although the P-/-→Fas+/+ chimeric animals appear to be well reconstituted (Table 2) and have a reasonable number of antigen-specific CD8+ T cells at the site of infection (Fig. 2), the availability of effector cells may be somewhat more limiting than in their non-chimeric P-/- counterparts. The net consequence is the slowed clearance of lytic virus in the chimeras, which in turn leads to greater damage resulting from Fas ligation by the P-/- CD8+ effectors. Thus the race between the virus and the immune effectors in this scenario favours the virus for too long, with the immune system trying to catch up, and many lung cells being damaged in the process. It is possible that the irradiation used to produce these chimeric animals may also increase Fas expression or otherwise alter lung physiology to enhance their susceptibility to Fas-mediated lysis. Exposure of the lung to chemotherapeutic agents like bleomycin results in an increase of Fas expression by lung epithelial cells (Kuwano et al., 1999 , 2000 ). In either case the suggestion is that Fas-mediated cytotoxicity is more promiscuous, causing proximal uninfected Fas+ cells to also be destroyed (Smyth et al., 1998 ).

Perforin-mediated cytotoxicity is generally more rapid and exquisitely targeted to the antigen-positive cells (Kagi et al., 1994 ; Lowin et al., 1994 b). For example, P-/- CTL do not read out in a conventional 6 h 51Cr-release assay while P+/+ CTL do (Kagi et al., 1994 ). The cell death mechanisms involving Fas are very old in the phylogenetic sense, though both the perforin/granzyme B (Kagi et al., 1994 ; Lowin et al., 1994 a , 1995 ; Mullbacher et al., 1999b ) and Fas-ligation pathways operate to initiate apoptosis via activation of the same caspases (Bird et al., 1998 ; Trapani et al., 1998 ). It seems clear that the rapid and efficient perforin-mediated cytotoxic pathway is the more desirable and efficient means for CTL to control this and other virus infections of the lung (Kagi et al., 1994 ; Topham et al., 1997 ). Perhaps the evolution of the perforin/granzyme cytotoxic pathway reflects the fact that Fas-directed CTLs cause too much collateral damage and are too dangerous to be used for normal virus control.

We thank Suzette Wingo and Mehdi Mehrpooya for technical assistance, Dr Anne-Marie Hamilton-Easton for advice on flow cytometry and Vicki Henderson for help with the manuscript. These experiments were supported by Public Health grant CA21765 and the American Lebanese Syrian Associated Charities. J.P.C. is the recipient of a fellowship from the Alfred Benzon Foundation, Denmark.
